description: Figure 1 presents X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns comparing the phase evolution of Fe-12Mn-9Al-3Cr-1.4C steels with 0.02 wt% and 0.04 wt% Nb under different aging conditions. Subfigure (a) displays patterns for 0.02Nb steels and subfigure (b) for 0.04Nb steels, each containing four curves representing as-cast condition and aging treatments at 723 K, 773 K, and 823 K for 3 hours. All patterns exhibit distinct diffraction peaks corresponding to body-centered cubic δ-ferrite [(110), (200), (211)] and face-centered cubic γ-austenite [(111), (200), (220), (311), (222)]. The consistent peak positions and intensities across all aging temperatures indicate that the dual-phase microstructure remains stable during heat treatment, with no significant phase transformation or new precipitates detectable by XRD. Comparative analysis between the two Nb contents reveals minimal differences in phase constitution, supporting the conclusion that Nb microalloying primarily influences grain refinement and precipitation behavior rather than altering the fundamental ferrite-austenite balance in these medium-Mn low-density steels.

This figure presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs illustrating the microstructural evolution of Nb-containing medium-Mn low-density steels with compositions Fe-12Mn-9Al-3Cr-1.4C-0.02Nb (panels a-d) and Fe-12Mn-9Al-3Cr-1.4C-0.04Nb (panels e-h). Subfigures (a) and (e) show the as-cast condition, while (b-d) and (f-h) display samples aged at 723 K, 773 K, and 823 K for 3 hours, respectively. All micrographs reveal a dual-phase structure with irregular-shaped δ-ferrite (yellow arrows) dispersed in a γ-austenite matrix. Submicron white NbC particles are indicated by purple arrows, appearing more frequently in the 0.04Nb steel. The images demonstrate that higher Nb content reduces austenite grain size by approximately half compared to the 0.02Nb steel due to NbC precipitation. Ferrite content remains relatively constant across aging temperatures (approximately 16-18 vol%), while the NbC particles slightly coarsen with increasing aging temperature. These microstructural features support the role of Nb in grain refinement and suppression of κ-carbide coarsening.

This figure presents a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) bright-field image showing a NbC precipitate within the austenite (γ) matrix of Fe-12Mn-9Al-3Cr-1.4C-0.02Nb low-density steel. The main micrograph displays a dark, submicron particle labeled "Spot 1" embedded in the γ matrix, with a scale bar indicating 500 nm. The inset shows a selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern acquired from Spot 1, revealing the crystallographic orientation relationship [100]$_\gamma$ // [100]$_{\text{NbC}}$ between the precipitate and matrix. Diffraction spots are indexed with red labels (0$\overline{2}$2, 002, 0$\overline{2}$0) and yellow labels (0$\overline{1}$1, 0$\overline{1}$1), confirming the coexistence of austenite and NbC phases. This characterization identifies the nano-scale NbC precipitation responsible for grain refinement and strengthening mechanisms in the 0.02Nb steel variant.
